q: Which blood group is known as the 'universal donor'?
explanation: Blood group O has no A or B antigens, so no reaction with recipient antibodies. O- is the true universal donor.

q: The ABO blood group system was discovered by which scientist?
explanation: Karl Landsteiner discovered ABO in 1900 (Nobel 1930). Also co-discovered Rh factor (1937) with Wiener.

q: Red blood cells do NOT contain which of the following?
explanation: Mature mammalian RBCs lack a nucleus (extruded during maturation) to accommodate more haemoglobin.

q: Which antibody crosses the placenta?
explanation: IgG is the only antibody that crosses placenta via FcRn receptor, providing passive immunity to newborn.

q: Vaccination provides which type of immunity?
explanation: Vaccine stimulates body to produce own antibodies and memory cells = artificial active immunity.

q: BCG vaccine protects against which disease?
explanation: BCG = Bacille Calmette-Guérin, live attenuated, against TB. Given at birth in India.

q: B-lymphocytes mature in which organ?
explanation: B-cells mature in bone marrow (B for Bone marrow). T-cells mature in thymus.

q: Most abundant WBC in human blood?
explanation: Neutrophils 60-70%, first responders to bacterial infection. Order: N>L>M>E>Basophils.

q: Tetanus vaccine is which type?
explanation: Tetanus toxoid = inactivated toxin. Stimulates antibodies against toxin, not bacterium.

q: Kala-azar is caused by which pathogen and vector?
explanation: Leishmania donovani (protozoan) transmitted by Phlebotomus sandfly. Bihar >70% of India's cases.

q: First antibody produced in primary immune response?
explanation: IgM is first produced (pentamer, largest). IgG comes later in secondary response.

q: Rh factor is named after which animal?
explanation: Landsteiner & Wiener discovered Rh factor (1937) using Rhesus monkey blood.

q: Match: 1.BCG 2.Tetanus 3.Hep B 4.Rabies
explanation: BCG=Live, Tetanus=Toxoid, Hep B=Recombinant(first,1986), Rabies=Inactivated(HDCV).

q: HIV primarily attacks which cells?
explanation: HIV binds CD4 receptor on Helper T cells, destroying immune coordinator cells → AIDS.

q: Erythroblastosis fetalis is caused by?
explanation: Rh- mother sensitized by Rh+ fetus → anti-Rh IgG crosses placenta → fetal RBC destruction. Prevented by Anti-D injection.

q: Maternal antibodies via breast milk provide which immunity?
explanation: IgA in colostrum + IgG via placenta = natural passive (pre-made antibodies, temporary, no memory).

q: India's Pulse Polio programme uses which vaccine?
explanation: OPV = oral polio vaccine (Sabin, live attenuated). Easier, gut immunity, cheaper. Risk: rare VDPV.

q: Which vitamin is essential for blood clotting?
explanation: Vitamin K needed for clotting factors II, VII, IX, X in liver. Newborns get K injection at birth.

q: Thymus gland is located in which part of body?
explanation: Thymus in mediastinum (upper chest, behind sternum). T-cell maturation. Shrinks after puberty.

q: Artificial passive immunity example?
explanation: RIG = pre-made antibodies injected = artificial passive. Immediate but temporary, no memory.

q: AES in Muzaffarpur (Bihar) linked to?
options: Litchi toxin (hypoglycin A, MCPG) causing hypoglycaemia
explanation: Unripe litchi toxins inhibit gluconeogenesis → hypoglycaemia in malnourished children. May-June season.

q: Blood clotting involves conversion of?
explanation: Thrombin converts soluble fibrinogen → insoluble fibrin mesh → traps RBCs → clot.

q: India declared polio-free in which year?
explanation: March 27, 2014 — WHO certified India and South-East Asia region polio-free. Last case: Jan 13, 2011.

q: Sickle cell anaemia mutation affects?
explanation: Point mutation: glutamic acid → valine at position 6 of β-globin. HbA→HbS. Autosomal recessive. Trait confers malaria resistance.

q: COVID-19 mRNA vaccines work by?
options: mRNA codes for spike protein; host cells produce it (Karikó & Weissman, Nobel 2023)
explanation: mRNA in lipid nanoparticles → host cells produce spike protein → antibody response. Does not enter nucleus or alter DNA.
